meta.notes: AI-generated proof-of-concept CPBS items for SpeedyCAT (chunk 5 of 10). NOT real MCAT/AAMC content and NOT from any answer key. Every stem and choice is plain text (no HTML/SVG/LaTeX). Answer keys were computed programmatically and each item passed a 4-criteria eval (four distinct choices; clean plain-text formatting; MCAT-appropriate difficulty; exactly one correct choice).

questions.stem: What ionic charge do elements in group 16 (oxygen group) most commonly form?
questions.explanation: Elements in group 16 (oxygen group) typically form ions with a charge of -2.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: What best explains the most common ionic charge a main-group nonmetal forms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It gains electrons until it reaches a full octet, forming a negative ion.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It loses all of its valence electrons, forming a large positive ion.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Its ionic charge always equals its row (period) number.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It shares electrons and never becomes an ion.

hints.rationale: Nonmetals complete their octet by gaining electrons, so they form negative ions.

hints.prompt: Group 16 atoms have 6 valence electrons. When they form their common ion, do they gain or lose electrons, and what is the sign of the charge?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They gain electrons, so the ion carries a negative charge.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They lose electrons, so the ion carries a positive charge.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They neither gain nor lose electrons.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They lose electrons but the ion is still negative.

hints.rationale: Group 16 nonmetals gain electrons to complete an octet, producing a negative ion.

hints.prompt: Which two of the original answer choices can be eliminated because group 16 ions are negative, not positive?
hints.rationale: Positive charges are impossible for these nonmetal ions, so the two positive options can be ruled out.

questions.stem: How much power is delivered if 1200 J of work is done in 10 s?
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Which equation defines power in terms of work and time?
hints.rationale: Power is the rate of doing work: work divided by time.

hints.prompt: 1200 J of work is done in 10 s. Which setup gives the power?
hints.rationale: Divide the work by the time to get power.

hints.prompt: Which listed answer is the trap you get by multiplying work by time instead of dividing?
hints.rationale: Multiplying 1200 by 10 gives 12000, the multiply-instead-of-divide trap.

questions.stem: Resistors of 47 Ω, 33 Ω are connected in series. What is the total resistance?
questions.explanation: Series resistances add: R_total = 47 + 33 = 80 Ω.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: For resistors connected in series, the total resistance is:

choices.label: B
choices.text: the product divided by the sum

choices.label: C
choices.text: the reciprocal of the sum of reciprocals

hints.rationale: Series resistances simply add.

hints.prompt: For 47 ohm and 33 ohm in series, which setup gives the total resistance?
hints.rationale: In series you add the two resistances.

hints.prompt: Which listed value would you get by mistakenly using the parallel-resistance formula?
hints.rationale: The parallel combination of 47 and 33 gives about 19.4 ohm, a trap for series problems.

questions.stem: What is the energy of a photon of wavelength 7 x 10^-7 m? (h = 6.63 x 10^-34 J·s, c = 3.0 x 10^8 m/s)
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Which equation gives a photon's energy from its wavelength?
hints.rationale: Photon energy equals Planck's constant times the speed of light divided by wavelength.

hints.prompt: With h = 6.63 x 10^-34 J·s, c = 3.0 x 10^8 m/s, and λ = 7 x 10^-7 m, which setup gives E?
hints.rationale: Multiply h and c, then divide by the wavelength.

hints.prompt: Which pair of options corresponds to mistakenly using twice or half the speed of light in E = hc/λ?
hints.rationale: Doubling c doubles E and halving c halves E, producing those two distractor values.

questions.stem: Overall, the molecule HCl is best described as
questions.explanation: HCl is a polar molecule because its dipoles do not cancel, giving a net dipole moment.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: What makes a diatomic molecule polar overall?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A difference in electronegativity between the two atoms creates a net dipole.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Being made of two atoms automatically makes it nonpolar.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Any molecule containing hydrogen is ionic.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only molecules with three or more atoms can be polar.

hints.rationale: Unequal electronegativity in a two-atom molecule gives a permanent net dipole.

hints.prompt: In HCl, how do the electronegativities of H and Cl compare, and what does that produce?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cl is more electronegative than H, producing a net dipole.

choices.label: B
choices.text: H and Cl have equal electronegativity, so any dipoles cancel.

choices.label: C
choices.text: H is far more electronegative, making the substance a metal.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The atoms are identical, so there is no dipole.

hints.rationale: The H-Cl electronegativity difference leaves a permanent separation of charge.

hints.prompt: Which two of the original choices can be eliminated because HCl is a molecular compound of two nonmetals?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A (an ionic compound) and B (a metallic solid)

choices.label: B
choices.text: C (a polar molecule) and D (a nonpolar molecule)

choices.label: C
choices.text: A (an ionic compound) and C (a polar molecule)

choices.label: D
choices.text: B (a metallic solid) and D (a nonpolar molecule)

hints.rationale: Two bonded nonmetals form neither an ionic compound nor a metallic solid.

questions.stem: What is the mass of 1 mol of CO2? (molar mass 44 g/mol)
questions.explanation: mass = moles x molar mass = 1 x 44 = 44 g.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: How do you find the mass of a sample from moles and molar mass?
hints.rationale: Mass equals the number of moles times the molar mass.

hints.prompt: For 1 mol of CO2 (molar mass 44 g/mol), which setup gives the mass?
hints.rationale: Multiply moles by molar mass.

hints.prompt: Which listed answer is the trap you get by dividing by the molar mass (finding moles) instead of multiplying?
hints.rationale: 1 divided by 44 gives about 0.0227, the inverted-operation trap.

questions.stem: If 0.05 L of 1 M solution is diluted to 1 L, what is the new concentration?
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Which relationship governs dilution (adding solvent to a solution)?
hints.rationale: The moles of solute are conserved: M1 V1 = M2 V2.

hints.prompt: Diluting 0.05 L of 1 M to 1 L, solving M1 V1 = M2 V2 for M2, which setup is correct?
hints.rationale: M2 equals the initial molarity times initial volume, divided by the final volume.

hints.prompt: Which listed answer is the trap from inverting the volume ratio (final over initial)?
hints.rationale: Using 1/0.05 gives 20, the flipped-ratio trap.

questions.stem: How much heat is needed to raise 250 g of water by 1 degrees C? (c = 4.18 J/(g·°C))
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Which equation gives the heat needed to change a substance's temperature?
hints.rationale: Heat equals mass times specific heat times temperature change.

hints.prompt: For 250 g of water, c = 4.18, and ΔT = 1, which setup gives q?
hints.rationale: Multiply mass, specific heat, and temperature change.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be eliminated because they use only one factor (mass alone, or c alone) instead of the full product?
hints.rationale: 250 J uses only the mass and 4.18 J uses only the specific heat, so both ignore the other factors.

questions.stem: An ideal fluid flows at 2 m/s through a pipe of cross-section 10 cm^2. What is its speed where the cross-section narrows to 2 cm^2?
questions.explanation: Continuity: A1 v1 = A2 v2, so v2 = (10)(2)/2 = 10 m/s.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: For an incompressible fluid, the continuity equation states:
hints.rationale: The volume flow rate A times v is constant along the pipe.

hints.prompt: With A1 = 10 cm^2, v1 = 2 m/s, and A2 = 2 cm^2, solving for v2, which setup is correct?
hints.rationale: v2 equals A1 v1 divided by A2.

hints.prompt: Which listed answer is the trap from inverting the area ratio (using A2/A1)?
hints.rationale: Using 2 x 2 / 10 gives 0.4, the flipped-area trap.

questions.stem: What is the maximum number of electrons that can occupy the n = 4 shell? (Use 2n^2.)
questions.explanation: Maximum electrons = 2n^2 = 2(4)^2 = 32.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: The maximum number of electrons that a shell with principal quantum number n can hold is:
hints.rationale: A shell holds up to 2n^2 electrons.

hints.prompt: For n = 4, which setup gives the maximum number of electrons?
hints.rationale: Square n, then multiply by 2.

hints.prompt: Which listed answer is the trap from using n^2 (forgetting the factor of 2)?
hints.rationale: 4^2 = 16 forgets to double, the missing-factor trap.

questions.stem: A surface has coefficient of friction 0.6 and normal force 200 N. What is the friction force?
questions.explanation: Friction f = μ N = (0.6)(200) = 120 N.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: The maximum friction force on a surface is given by:
hints.rationale: Friction force equals the coefficient of friction times the normal force.

hints.prompt: With μ = 0.6 and N = 200 N, which setup gives the friction force?
hints.rationale: Multiply the coefficient by the normal force.

hints.prompt: Which listed answer is the trap from dividing the normal force by μ instead of multiplying?
hints.rationale: 200 / 0.6 is about 333, the divide-instead-of-multiply trap.

questions.stem: Two resistors, 10 Ω and 2 Ω, are connected in parallel. What is the equivalent resistance?
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: For two resistors in parallel, the equivalent resistance is:
hints.rationale: Two parallel resistors combine as the product over the sum.

hints.prompt: For 10 ohm and 2 ohm in parallel, which setup gives the equivalent resistance?
hints.rationale: Use product over sum for two parallel resistors.

hints.prompt: Which listed answer is the trap you get by adding the resistances (the series result)?
hints.rationale: 10 + 2 = 12 is the series sum, wrong for a parallel combination.

questions.stem: Light travels through a medium of refractive index 1.6. What is its speed in that medium? (c = 3.0 x 10^8 m/s)
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: The speed of light in a medium of refractive index n is:
hints.rationale: Light slows in a medium: its speed is c divided by the index.

hints.prompt: With c = 3.0 x 10^8 m/s and n = 1.6, which setup gives the speed in the medium?
hints.rationale: Divide the vacuum speed by the refractive index.

hints.prompt: Which listed answer is the trap from multiplying c by n instead of dividing?
hints.rationale: 3.0 x 10^8 times 1.6 gives 4.8 x 10^8, faster than light, so it must be wrong.

questions.stem: What is the strongest type of intermolecular force present in pure CO2?
questions.explanation: The strongest intermolecular force in CO2 is London dispersion forces.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Hydrogen bonding occurs only when hydrogen is bonded directly to which atoms?
hints.rationale: Hydrogen bonds require H bonded to nitrogen, oxygen, or fluorine.

hints.prompt: CO2 is a nonpolar molecule with no H bonded to N, O, or F. Which intermolecular forces are ruled out?
hints.rationale: With no suitable H and no ions, hydrogen bonding and ionic bonding cannot occur.

hints.prompt: Which two of the original choices can be eliminated for pure CO2?

choices.label: B
choices.text: A (dipole-dipole forces) and D (London dispersion forces)

choices.label: D
choices.text: B (hydrogen bonding) and D (London dispersion forces)

hints.rationale: CO2 has no H bonded to N/O/F and forms no ions, so those two options are impossible.

questions.stem: How many molecules are in 1.5 mol of a substance? (Avogadro's number = 6.022 x 10^23 /mol)
questions.explanation: molecules = moles x N_A = 1.5 x 6.022 x 10^23 = 9.03 x 10^23.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: To convert a number of moles into a number of molecules, you:
hints.rationale: Number of molecules equals moles times Avogadro's number.

hints.prompt: For 1.5 mol (N_A = 6.022 x 10^23 /mol), which setup gives the number of molecules?
hints.rationale: Multiply the moles by Avogadro's number.

hints.prompt: Which listed answer is the trap from dividing by Avogadro's number instead of multiplying?
hints.rationale: 1.5 divided by 6.022 x 10^23 gives about 2.49 x 10^-24, the inverted-operation trap.

questions.stem: A solution has pH = 5. What is its hydrogen ion concentration [H+]?
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: How is the hydrogen-ion concentration obtained from pH?
hints.rationale: [H+] is ten raised to the negative pH.

hints.prompt: For pH = 5, which setup gives [H+]?
hints.rationale: Raise 10 to the negative of the pH value.

hints.prompt: Which listed answer has the wrong sign in the exponent (10^(+5) instead of 10^(-5))?
hints.rationale: 1 x 10^5 M drops the negative sign on the exponent, a huge unphysical concentration.

questions.stem: Adding 20900 J of heat to 250 g of water raises its temperature by how much? (c = 4.18 J/(g·°C))
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Rearranging q = m c ΔT to find the temperature change gives:
hints.rationale: Divide the heat by the product of mass and specific heat.

hints.prompt: With q = 20900 J, m = 250 g, and c = 4.18, which setup gives ΔT?
hints.rationale: Divide the heat by mass times specific heat.

hints.prompt: Which listed answer is the trap from dividing by the mass only and forgetting the specific heat?
hints.rationale: 20900 / 250 = 83.6 omits the specific heat c, a common trap.

questions.stem: An ideal fluid flows at 1 m/s through a pipe of cross-sectional area 10 m^2. What is the volume flow rate?
questions.explanation: Flow rate Q = A v = (10)(1) = 10 m^3/s.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: The volume flow rate of a fluid through a pipe equals:
hints.rationale: Flow rate is cross-sectional area times flow speed.

hints.prompt: With A = 10 m^2 and v = 1 m/s, which setup gives the volume flow rate?
hints.rationale: Multiply the area by the speed.

hints.prompt: Which listed answer is the trap from adding area and speed instead of multiplying?
hints.rationale: 10 + 1 = 11 is the add-instead-of-multiply trap.

questions.stem: What is the maximum number of electrons in a f subshell?
questions.explanation: A f subshell holds at most 14 electrons.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: The maximum number of electrons a subshell can hold equals:

choices.label: A
choices.text: 2 times the number of orbitals in that subshell

hints.rationale: Each orbital holds 2 electrons, so a subshell holds 2 times its number of orbitals.

hints.prompt: An f subshell has 7 orbitals. Using 2 electrons per orbital, which setup gives its capacity?
hints.rationale: Multiply 2 electrons per orbital by 7 f orbitals.

hints.prompt: Which two listed options are actually the capacities of the s and p subshells (not f)?
hints.rationale: 2 is the s-subshell capacity and 6 is the p-subshell capacity, neither is the f capacity.

questions.stem: An object moves in a circle of radius 10 m at 8 m/s. What is its centripetal acceleration?
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Centripetal acceleration for circular motion is given by:
hints.rationale: Centripetal acceleration is speed squared divided by radius.

hints.prompt: With v = 8 m/s and r = 10 m, which setup gives the centripetal acceleration?
hints.rationale: Square the speed, then divide by the radius.

hints.prompt: Which listed answer is the trap from multiplying v^2 by r instead of dividing?
hints.rationale: 8^2 x 10 = 640, the multiply-instead-of-divide trap.

questions.stem: A 2 μF capacitor is charged to 5 V. How much charge does it store?
questions.explanation: Q = C V = (2 μF)(5 V) = 10 μC.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: The charge stored on a capacitor is given by:
hints.rationale: Charge equals capacitance times voltage.

hints.prompt: With C = 2 μF and V = 5 V, which setup gives the stored charge?
hints.rationale: Multiply capacitance by voltage.

hints.prompt: Which listed answer is the trap from adding C and V instead of multiplying?
hints.rationale: 2 + 5 = 7 is the add-instead-of-multiply trap.

questions.stem: An object is 12 cm from a thin converging lens of focal length 20 cm. Where is the image formed?
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: The thin-lens equation relates focal length, object distance, and image distance as:
hints.rationale: The reciprocals of object and image distance add to the reciprocal of the focal length.

hints.prompt: With do = 12 cm and f = 20 cm, solving 1/f = 1/do + 1/di for 1/di, which is correct?
hints.rationale: Subtract 1/do from 1/f to isolate 1/di.

hints.prompt: Which listed answer is the trap of simply copying the focal length as the image distance?
hints.rationale: 20 cm merely repeats the focal length, ignoring the lens equation.

questions.stem: CO2 has polar bonds but is a nonpolar molecule because

choices.label: A
choices.text: carbon and oxygen have identical electronegativity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: it has lone pairs on the central carbon.

choices.label: C
choices.text: it contains only nonpolar covalent bonds.

choices.label: D
choices.text: its linear, symmetric shape makes the bond dipoles cancel.

questions.explanation: The two C=O dipoles point oppositely in the linear molecule and cancel, giving no net dipole.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: How can a molecule that contains polar bonds still be nonpolar overall?

choices.label: A
choices.text: If its shape is symmetric so the bond dipoles cancel.

choices.label: B
choices.text: If it has lone pairs on the central atom.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Only if all of its bonds are nonpolar.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A molecule with polar bonds can never be nonpolar.

hints.rationale: Symmetric geometry can make individual bond dipoles cancel to zero net dipole.

hints.prompt: CO2 is linear (O=C=O). What happens to its two C=O bond dipoles?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They point in opposite directions and cancel.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They add together to give a large net dipole.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They vanish because the bonds are actually nonpolar.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They create lone pairs on the carbon atom.

hints.rationale: In a linear O=C=O the two equal dipoles oppose and cancel.

hints.prompt: Which two of the original choices are false statements that can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: B (it has lone pairs on the central carbon) and C (it contains only nonpolar covalent bonds)

choices.label: B
choices.text: A (carbon and oxygen have identical electronegativity) and D (its linear, symmetric shape makes the bond dipoles cancel)

choices.label: C
choices.text: A (carbon and oxygen have identical electronegativity) and C (it contains only nonpolar covalent bonds)

choices.label: D
choices.text: B (it has lone pairs on the central carbon) and D (its linear, symmetric shape makes the bond dipoles cancel)

hints.rationale: Carbon has no lone pairs in CO2, and the C=O bonds are polar, so both statements are false.

questions.stem: How many moles correspond to 1.81 x 10^24 molecules? (Avogadro's number = 6.022 x 10^23 /mol)
questions.explanation: moles = molecules / N_A = 1.81 x 10^24 / (6.022 x 10^23) = 3 mol.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: To convert a number of molecules into moles, you:
hints.rationale: Moles equal the number of molecules divided by Avogadro's number.

hints.prompt: For 1.81 x 10^24 molecules (N_A = 6.022 x 10^23 /mol), which setup gives the number of moles?
hints.rationale: Divide the molecule count by Avogadro's number.

hints.prompt: Which listed answer is the trap from multiplying by Avogadro's number instead of dividing?
hints.rationale: Multiplying the two large numbers gives about 1.09 x 10^48, an impossibly large mole count.

questions.stem: What is the pH of a solution with [H+] = 1 x 10^-5 M?
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: pH is defined as:
hints.rationale: pH is the negative base-10 logarithm of the hydrogen-ion concentration.

hints.prompt: For [H+] = 1 x 10^-5 M, which setup gives the pH?
hints.rationale: Take the negative logarithm of the concentration.

hints.prompt: Which listed answer is actually the pOH (14 minus the pH), not the pH?
hints.rationale: 14 - 5 = 9 is the pOH, a trap for the pH question.

questions.stem: How many grams of water are heated if 4180 J raises the temperature by 5 degrees C? (c = 4.18 J/(g·°C))
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Solving q = m c ΔT for the mass gives:
hints.rationale: Divide the heat by the product of specific heat and temperature change.

hints.prompt: With q = 4180 J, c = 4.18, and ΔT = 5, which setup gives the mass?
hints.rationale: Divide the heat by c times the temperature change.

hints.prompt: Which listed answer is the trap from multiplying the quantities instead of dividing?
hints.rationale: 4180 x 5 = 20900 multiplies instead of dividing, a clear trap.

questions.stem: Entropy is best described as a measure of

choices.label: B
choices.text: the total heat content of a system.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the disorder or number of accessible microstates of a system.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the pressure exerted by a gas.

questions.explanation: Entropy quantifies disorder/multiplicity; gases have higher entropy than liquids or solids.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Which statement about entropy is correct?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A gas has higher entropy than the same substance as a solid.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Entropy and temperature are the same quantity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Entropy measures the pressure of a gas.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Entropy is the total heat stored in a system.

hints.rationale: Entropy tracks disorder, so a gas (more disordered) has higher entropy than a solid.

hints.prompt: Two samples differ only in molecular arrangement: one highly ordered, one highly random. Which has greater entropy?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Entropy depends only on temperature, so it cannot be compared.

hints.rationale: Greater molecular randomness means more accessible microstates and higher entropy.

hints.prompt: Which two of the original choices can be eliminated because they name other thermodynamic quantities?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A (the temperature of a system) and D (the pressure exerted by a gas)

choices.label: B
choices.text: B (the total heat content of a system) and C (the disorder or number of accessible microstates of a system)

choices.label: C
choices.text: A (the temperature of a system) and B (the total heat content of a system)

choices.label: D
choices.text: C (the disorder or number of accessible microstates of a system) and D (the pressure exerted by a gas)

hints.rationale: Temperature and pressure are distinct properties, not what entropy measures.

questions.stem: What is the atomic number of oxygen?
questions.explanation: The atomic number equals the number of protons; oxygen has 8.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: What does an element's atomic number equal?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The number of protons in its nucleus.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The number of protons plus neutrons.

hints.rationale: The atomic number is defined as the proton count.

hints.prompt: How do you determine oxygen's atomic number from the periodic table?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Read the whole number (its proton count) shown for O.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Add its protons and neutrons together.

hints.rationale: The atomic number is listed directly and equals the proton count.

hints.prompt: Which two listed values are far too small to be oxygen's proton count?
hints.rationale: Oxygen is well past the first few elements, so 4 and 5 are too small.

questions.stem: A spring with force constant 500 N/m is stretched 0.2 m. What restoring force does it exert?
questions.explanation: Hooke's law: F = k x = (500)(0.2) = 100 N.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Hooke's law gives the restoring force of a spring as:
hints.rationale: The spring force equals the force constant times the displacement.

hints.prompt: With k = 500 N/m and x = 0.2 m, which setup gives the restoring force?
hints.rationale: Multiply the force constant by the stretch distance.

hints.prompt: Which listed answer is the trap from dividing k by x instead of multiplying?
hints.rationale: 500 / 0.2 = 2500 is the divide-instead-of-multiply trap.

questions.stem: Two parallel plates 0.02 m apart have a potential difference of 12 V. What is the electric field between them?
questions.explanation: For parallel plates, E = V / d = 12 / 0.02 = 600 V/m.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: The uniform electric field between two parallel plates is:
hints.rationale: Field strength equals the potential difference divided by the plate separation.

hints.prompt: With V = 12 V and d = 0.02 m, which setup gives the field?
hints.rationale: Divide the voltage by the separation.

hints.prompt: Which listed answer is the trap from multiplying V by d instead of dividing?
hints.rationale: 12 x 0.02 = 0.24 multiplies instead of dividing, a clear trap.

questions.stem: For a lens, the object distance is 10 cm and the image distance is 20 cm. What is the magnification?
questions.explanation: Magnification m = -di/do = -20/10 = -2.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Lens magnification is given by:
hints.rationale: Magnification equals the negative of image distance over object distance.
